findings.description: Email/SMTP header injection (CWE-93): msg.to and msg.subject are interpolated into raw CRLF-delimited headers with no newline sanitization, letting a crafted subject/to inject Bcc/Cc headers or body content; the imported MIMEText/EmailMessage (which would escape headers) is unused.
Subject header is built via f-string interpolation of attacker-influenceable msg.subject without stripping/rejecting CR/LF, the primary header-injection sink feeding sendmail.
sendmail passes unvalidated msg.to as the envelope recipient (and the same tainted value reaches the To: header) with no RFC 5321/5322 address validation, allowing envelope/recipient manipulation.

Dedup keys only on msg.to, so a distinct security-relevant email to a recipient can be silently dropped if any prior message to that same address was queued (notification/delivery suppression vector); key should hash subject+body.
SMTP submission on port 587 is opened in cleartext with no server.starttls() and no authentication, transmitting bodies and recipients unencrypted (CWE-319).
smtplib.SMTP is created without a timeout, so an unresponsive/malicious SMTP server can hang flush() indefinitely across the pending loop (CWE-400, availability/DoS).
A new SMTP connection is opened and torn down per message inside the flush loop instead of reusing one connection; performance concern deferred to sibling critic.
